name: El Cabril Route
name_original: Ruta del Cabril — circular desde Xinestosu
region: National Park Fuentes del Narcea Degaña e Ibias
description: This difficult 9.0-mile (14.4 km) jogging route from Xinestosu offers challenging terrain and panoramic views in Asturias.
region_intro: Jogging around National Park Fuentes del Narcea Degaña e Ibias offers diverse terrain within a protected natural landscape. The region is characterized by ancient forests, including the extensive Muniellos oak forest, and a mountainous landscape with significant elevation changes. River valleys and glacial lagoons provide picturesque views and flatter sections, while peaks like Cueto de Arbas offer more challenging ascents. This varied environment supports a network of trails suitable for running. Best jogging routes around National Park Fuentes del Narcea Degaña e Ibias The most popular jogging route is El Cabril Route, a 9.0 miles (14.4 km) trail that takes about 2 hours 34 minutes to complete. This difficult route features substantial elevation gain through forested areas. Another top favourite among local runners is Running loop from Oubachu, a difficult 4.5 miles (7.2 km) path. This route includes varied terrain with notable ascents and descents. Local runners also love the Brañutín – Cuíña loop from Porto de Ancares, a 4.2 miles (6.8 km) trail leading through mountainous landscapes, often completed in about 1 hour 8 minutes. Jogging around National Park Fuentes del Narcea Degaña e Ibias is defined by ancient forests, mountainous terrain, and river valleys. The network offers options for experienced trail runners seeking challenging routes. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 60 runners have used komoot to explore National Park Fuentes del Narcea Degaña e Ibias's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on running routes in National Park Fuentes del Narcea Degaña e Ibias?
A: The National Park Fuentes del Narcea, Degaña e Ibias offers a diverse and challenging landscape for runners. You'll encounter ancient forests, including some of the best-preserved mature forests in the Iberian Peninsula, and mountainous terrain with significant elevation changes. River valleys and glacial lagoons provide picturesque views and flatter sections, while peaks like Cueto de Arbas offer more challenging ascents. The routes are generally difficult, featuring substantial elevation gain and varied surfaces.

Q: Are there any circular running routes available in the park?
A: Yes, the park features several circular running routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31456973" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Running loop from Oubachu&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 4.5-mile (7.2 km) path with notable ascents and descents. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38170641" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Brañutín – Cuíña loop from Porto de Ancares&lt;/a&gt;, a 4.2-mile (6.8 km) trail through mountainous landscapes. For a longer challenge, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1055958413/branas-de-la-seita-zaramedo-and-degollada-loop-from-palacios-del-sil" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Brañas de la Seita, Zaramedo, and Degollada Loop from Palacios del Sil&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 15 km.

Q: What are some notable natural features or landmarks I might see while running in the park?
A: Runners in the National Park Fuentes del Narcea, Degaña e Ibias can experience stunning natural beauty. You'll pass through ancient forests like the Muniellos oak forest and the Monasterio de Hermo beech forest. The park also features the headwaters of the Narcea and Ibias rivers, and glacial lagoons. You might also encounter scenic mountain passes such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5593878" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozo de las Mujeres Muertas Pass&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5593872" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Connio Pass&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5371946" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Leitariegos&lt;/a&gt;, which offer panoramic views.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly running paths in the National Park Fuentes del Narcea Degaña e Ibias?
A: The running routes listed for National Park Fuentes del Narcea, Degaña e Ibias are generally classified as &lt;strong&gt;difficult&lt;/strong&gt; due to significant elevation changes and varied terrain. While the park offers extensive trails, many are suited for experienced trail runners. For beginners, it's advisable to seek out flatter sections along riverbanks or consult local information centers for less strenuous options, though specific 'easy' routes are not highlighted in the main guide.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running trails in National Park Fuentes del Narcea Degaña e Ibias?
A: While the National Park Fuentes del Narcea, Degaña e Ibias is a natural area, specific regulations regarding dogs on trails can vary. It's generally recommended to keep dogs on a leash to protect local wildlife, especially in areas known for brown bears and Cantabrian capercaillie. Always check with local park authorities or interpretation centers for the most current rules on pet access before heading out.

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in National Park Fuentes del Narcea Degaña e Ibias?
A: The best time to go running in National Park Fuentes del Narcea, Degaña e Ibias is typically during spring, summer, and early autumn. These seasons offer more favorable weather conditions, with less snow and ice on higher elevations, making trails more accessible. Spring brings lush greenery, while autumn showcases vibrant fall foliage. Always check the local weather forecast before your run, as mountain weather can change rapidly.

Q: Are there any facilities or interpretation centers in the park that can help with route planning?
A: Yes, the park has several facilities to assist visitors. You can find information centers such as the Casa del Parque in Courias and the Interpretation Center in Cerredo. These centers provide maps and detailed information on routes, helping you plan your jogging excursions effectively. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6276468" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Muniellos Interpretation Center&lt;/a&gt; also offers insights into the pristine environment of the Muniellos reserve.

Q: Is public transport available to or within National Park Fuentes del Narcea Degaña e Ibias?
A: Public transport options directly to or extensively within the National Park Fuentes del Narcea, Degaña e Ibias are limited due to its remote and protected nature. Most visitors access the park by car. It's advisable to plan your journey with private transport to reach trailheads and various points of interest. Check local bus schedules for towns like Cangas del Narcea, but expect to drive to reach the actual running routes.

Q: Where can I find parking for the running trails in the park?
A: Parking facilities are typically available near popular trailheads and villages within or on the periphery of the National Park Fuentes del Narcea, Degaña e Ibias. For example, if you plan to explore areas near the Muniellos forest, there will be designated parking. Always look for marked parking areas and respect local regulations, especially in protected zones, to ensure you don't obstruct access or damage the environment.

Q: What is the longest running route featured in this guide?
A: The longest running route highlighted in this guide is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1055958413/branas-de-la-seita-zaramedo-and-degollada-loop-from-palacios-del-sil" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Brañas de la Seita, Zaramedo, and Degollada Loop from Palacios del Sil&lt;/a&gt;. This challenging route spans approximately 15.6 kilometers (9.7 miles) and involves significant elevation gain, making it suitable for experienced runners.

Q: Are there any permits required for accessing certain areas or trails in the park?
A: Yes, access to certain highly protected areas within the National Park, such as the core Muniellos Integral Nature Reserve, is strictly controlled. Human access is limited to a small number of visitors daily (e.g., 20) and requires a permit booked in advance. While many general running trails do not require permits, it's crucial to check for specific restrictions, especially if you plan to venture into the most pristine or sensitive ecological zones.

Q: What do other runners say about their experience in National Park Fuentes del Narcea Degaña e Ibias?
A: Over 60 runners have used komoot to explore the varied terrain of National Park Fuentes del Narcea, Degaña e Ibias. They often praise the region's ancient forests, mountainous landscapes, and the sense of wilderness. The challenging nature of the routes, with their significant elevation changes, is frequently highlighted as a key aspect of the running experience, appealing to those seeking a demanding and rewarding trail run.
tips: The first lagoon can be seen by turning aside a few meters from the path. Of the three lagoons in the complex, one is permanent and the other seasonal.

The town of Xinestosu (in Spanish Genestoso) is famous for making an excellent artisanal cheese, made in esparto moulds. There is a store where you can buy this cheese, they also explain the production process, etc.

faqs.question: Where does the Route of Cabril trail start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The trail begins in the village of Xinestosu (also known as Genestoso). You'll find parking options within the village, which serves as the main access point for this circular route.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this jogging route?
faqs.answer: While the komoot estimate for jogging is around 2 hours and 34 minutes, the challenging terrain and significant elevation gain mean most people will take longer. For hiking, it's typically 5 to 5.5 hours. For jogging, expect to alternate between running and walking, especially on steeper sections, so plan for a demanding effort that could take several hours depending on your pace and fitness.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Route of Cabril?
faqs.answer: The terrain is quite varied and demanding. You'll encounter sections of track suitable for a 4x4 vehicle, steep passes, and tight routes. There's a notable strong and stony climb leading to El Colláu. While there's a flatter, scenic 5-kilometer section, overall, expect rocky ascents and varied ground underfoot.

faqs.question: Is the Route of Cabril — circular from Xinestosu dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail is generally dog-friendly. However, given that it traverses a genuine rural environment with pastures and potentially livestock, it's always best to keep your dog on a leash to ensure their safety and respect local wildlife and farming activities.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to jog this route?
faqs.answer: The best times are typically spring, summer, and autumn. In winter, the trail can be considerably more difficult and even impassable due to snow, especially at higher altitudes nearing 2000 meters. Always check local weather conditions before you go.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or is there an entrance fee for the Natural Park of Fuentes de Narcea, Degaña e Ibias?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits required or entrance fees to access the trails within the Natural Park of Fuentes de Narcea, Degaña e Ibias, including the Route of Cabril. However, always respect park regulations, such as staying on marked paths and not disturbing wildlife.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers spectacular panoramic views. As you ascend, you'll be treated to impressive vistas of the Xinestosu Valley and the pastures of Somiedo. From the highest points, on clear days, you might even see the coast of Galicia and Asturias. A notable highlight is the Tres Lagunas Pass.

faqs.question: Does this trail connect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, a significant portion of this route, approximately 38%, coincides with a section of the Transcantábrica trail. Additionally, part of the route also aligns with the GR.203 "Por donde camino el oso" trail.

faqs.question: Is the Route of Cabril suitable for beginner joggers?
faqs.answer: Given its 'difficult' grading for jogging, significant elevation gain (over 760 meters), and varied, challenging terrain including stony climbs, this route is not recommended for beginner joggers. It requires a good level of fitness and experience with trail running.

faqs.question: Are there any local products or amenities near the trailhead in Xinestosu?
faqs.answer: The village of Xinestosu (Genestoso) is famous for its excellent handmade cheese, traditionally made in esparto molds, which has an intense flavor. It's worth exploring the village for local products after your run.

faqs.question: What is the highest point reached on the trail?
faqs.answer: The trail reaches altitudes nearing 2000 meters, providing constant and breathtaking views along the mountain ridges.
